question_title: Which range will the Europe Brent spot price (USD per barrel) fall in on April 30, 2026?
summary: Brent crude oil is experiencing extreme volatility due to the US-Israel war on Iran (started Feb 28, 2026). Prices swung from ~$70 pre-war to $120 intraday on March 9, then back to $85 on March 10 after Trump said the war would end "very soon." The key variable is whether the Strait of Hormuz (20% of global supply) reopens before April 30 — 50 days away. June 2026 Brent futures at $85.33 provide the strongest market anchor. EIA forecasts >$95 for the next 2 months, then <$80 in Q3, placing April 30 near a transition point. Multiple bearish forces (SPR releases, bearish fundamentals, ceasefire back-channels) compete with bullish forces (active war, Hormuz closure, 6.7 mbpd shut in). The distribution is wide and somewhat bimodal between a resolution scenario ($70-85) and a continued-conflict scenario ($85-100+).

factors.description: June 2026 Brent futures trading at $85.33 in backwardation - the market's probability-weighted best estimate for the late April/May timeframe. This is the single strongest calibration anchor, incorporating all known information about war trajectory, SPR releases, and supply/demand.
EIA STEO forecasts Brent >$95/b for next 2 months (March-April), then falling below $80 in Q3 2026. April 30 sits at the transition point, likely still elevated but beginning to decline.

Iran rejects ceasefire, FM vows to continue fighting. Strait of Hormuz effectively closed. 6.7 mbpd Middle East production shut in. War is only 10 days old with no end in sight. Conflict has spread across the region.

Trump says war will end 'very soon'. Iran reached out to CIA via backchannel. Khamenei assassinated (decapitated leadership). Historical precedent: 2025 Iran-Israel Twelve-Day War ended with ceasefire quickly. 50 days provides substantial time for resolution.
G7/IEA coordinating largest-ever strategic petroleum reserve release. SPR releases historically cap prices effectively. OPEC+ has spare capacity that could be deployed.
JP Morgan maintains $60/bbl average forecast for 2026 despite war - underlying supply/demand fundamentals are bearish (global oversupply). Pre-war Brent was ~$70.
Extreme volatility: 13.8% single-day drop on March 10, prices went $70→$120→$85 in 10 days, showing strong mean-reversion tendency and market pricing in rapid normalization.

War escalation risk: conflict has already spread to Cyprus, Saudi Arabia, UAE, Iraq, Kuwait, Qatar. Ground invasion discussed. If Hormuz remains closed for months, sustained $100+ prices are plausible.

reasoning:
My forecast for the Brent spot price on April 30, 2026:

| Range | Probability |
|-------|------------|
| Below 70.00 | 7% |
| 70.00 to below 85.00 | 35% |
| 85.00 to below 100.00 | 35% |
| 100.00 or higher | 23% |

**Key reasoning:**

The US-Israel war on Iran (started Feb 28) has caused unprecedented oil market volatility. Brent swung from ~$70 pre-war to $120 intraday on March 9, settling at $85 on March 10. The primary anchor is the **June 2026 Brent futures at $85.33**, placing the market's expected value right at the boundary between the two middle buckets.

The forecast is driven by a near-bimodal distribution reflecting two competing scenarios:
- **Resolution scenario (~42%)**: Trump wants a quick end, Iran's leadership is decapitated, CIA backchannel exists, SPR releases pending → prices fall to $70-85
- **Continued conflict scenario (~51%)**: Hormuz remains closed, 6.7 mbpd shut in, Iran rejects ceasefire, war spreading → prices stay $85-100+
- **Tail scenarios (~7%)**: Full normalization below $70 only if war ends very quickly with massive SPR release
